attributes.stem:
A 54-year-old man presents with a one-year history of headaches and back pain. His wife has also noticed a deepening of his voice and frequent snoring. Upon further questioning, he revealed an increase in shoe size, along with enlargement of the nose and development of frontal bossing over the years.

During the examination, his blood pressure is measured at 168/82 mmHg and pulse 112/min. Macroglossia and pharynx crowding are noted, along with excessive sweating. Tinel's sign is found to be positive bilaterally. An MRI of the pituitary reveals a 14 mm pituitary mass without parasellar extension.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient is diagnosed with acromegaly, an endocrine disorder characterised by the dysregulated hypersecretion of growth hormone (GH), resulting in an excessive production of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1). This condition is typically caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma. The direct compressive effects of the tumour mass give rise to symptoms such as headaches and bitemporal hemianopia when the optic chasm is compromised. The excess GH and IGF-1 contribute to the overgrowth of various tissues, including connective tissue, cartilage, bone, skin, and visceral organs.

The primary treatment for acromegaly patients is usually transsphenoidal surgery for the resection of the pituitary adenoma. In specialised referral centres, remission can be achieved in approximately 80-90% of microadenomas (<1 cm) and around 50-75% of macroadenomas (>1 cm). Follow up medical treatment is often needed as given the extent and spread of the tumour by the time of diagnosis it is difficult to completely clear it by surgery.

Medical therapy is indicated for patients with persistent disease post-surgery or for cases where complete resection of the tumour is unlikely, such as those invading the cavernous sinus or the floor of the sella turcica. Somatostatin receptor ligands (SRL), including octreotide (available subcutaneously or intramuscularly) and lanreotide (available subcutaneously), are typically administered on a monthly basis. Oral octreotide is approved for treating acromegaly in patients who are well-controlled on injectable SRL.

Pegvisomant, a human GH receptor antagonist, competes with endogenous GH for binding at its receptor and blocks peripheral production of IGF-1. However, it does not target the GH-secreting pituitary tumor, and thus, GH hypersecretion continues during drug administration, and tumour shrinkage does not occur. It is generally employed as a second-line therapy for patients who do not achieve biochemical control with maximum doses of SRL.

Cabergoline is not often used as it is not effective very much. It is a relatively long-acting dopamine agonist, offers the advantage of being cost-effective and administered orally, as opposed to SRL. Its effect on inducing biochemical control is relatively modest. It can be considered as the first-line adjuvant medical therapy post-surgery for patients with only modest elevations of serum IGF-1 and mild signs and symptoms of GH excess.

Radiation therapy, including stereotactic radiotherapy, is considered an adjuvant treatment for patients with aggressive adenomas that remain uncured by surgery and are resistant to medical treatment. It may result in biochemical control, reducing the need for lifelong medical therapy. However, the complete therapeutic effect may take several years to manifest. Medical therapy is necessary while waiting for the response to radiation therapy.
learningPoints: The primary treatment for acromegaly patients is usually transsphenoidal surgery for the resection of the pituitary adenoma.

Medical therapy is indicated for patients with persistent disease post-surgery or for cases where complete resection of the tumour is unlikely, such as those invading the cavernous sinus or the floor of the sella turcica.
references: Katznelson L, Laws Jr ER, Melmed S, Molitch ME, Murad MH, Utz A, et al. Acromegaly: an endocrine society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab [Internet]. 2014 Nov 1 [cited 2023 Aug 13];99(11):3933-51. Available from: https://academic.oup.com/jcem/article/99/11/3933/2836347 doi: 10.1210/jc.2014-2700

Giustina A, Barkhoudarian G, Beckers A, Ben-Shlomo A, Biermasz N, Biller B, et al. Multidisciplinary management of acromegaly: a consensus. Rev Endocr Metab Disord [Internet]. 2020 Dec [cited 2023 Aug 13];21:667-78. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7942783/ doi: 10.1007/s11154-020-09588-z

Ogedegbe OJ, Cheema AY, Khan MA, Junaid SZ, Erebo JK, Ayirebi-Acquah E, et al. A Comprehensive Review of Four Clinical Practice Guidelines of Acromegaly. Cureus [Internet]. 2022 Sep 3 [cited 2023 Aug 13];14(9). Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9453869/ doi: 10.7759/cureus.28722
